stories.title: Chicago/Midwest People & Company News, week of July 24, 2026
stories.excerpt: CBRE announced that Laurel Keller has joined the firm as a first vice president in its Columbus office for CBRE Hotels. In this role, Keller works alongside Eric Belfrage and Michael Shirey, specializing in hotel investment sales nationwide and advising clients on acquisitions and dispositions while optimizing real estate value and investment performance. Based in the Midwest, she serves hospitality investors, owners and operators across the country. CBRE also announced that Ajay Kalra has been appointed managing director and market leader for its Omaha, Nebraska, business, in addition to his role overseeing the firm’s Iowa business. In this expanded role, Kalra will be responsible for all day-to-day operations for the company’s advisory services in Omaha and Iowa, including leasing, sales, valuations, and debt and structured finance across all property types. The appointment follows the transition of Bennett Ginsberg, formerly Omaha market leader, to a full-time producer role with CBRE. Ambrose announced key promotions and new hires across its growing team. The announcement follows the recent hiring of Courtney Schneider as vice president and market officer for the Mountain States region. Clay Smith has been promoted to director of development, and Kendrick Crisp has been promoted to accounting manager. Ambrose also welcomed new hires, including Anne Stewart, who has joined Ambrose as chief of staff, supporting founder & CEO Aasif Bade in advancing key strategic priorities across the organization. Leslie Hobbs has joined as vice president of accounting, while Sean Steidler has joined as vice president of financial planning and analysis. Additionally, Natalie Peters has joined as director of investor relations, Jake Okerson has joined as an accountant, and Melodee Allen has joined as an accounting operations specialist. Cathy Roth has also joined Ambrose as office and operations coordinator, where she will oversee day-to-day office operations. JLL announced the
bullets: CBRE elevated leadership in Columbus (Keller), Omaha (Kalra) for advisory growth.
Ambrose hired VP Mountain States officer (Schneider), expanded finance/IR team.
Staffing moves signal broker confidence in Midwest and Mountain markets.
stories.summary: CBRE and Ambrose announced leadership promotions and new hires across the Midwest and Mountain regions, including new market leaders in Columbus and Omaha, and expanded finance and strategic roles at Ambrose. These appointments signal sustained institutional commitment to grow advisory services in secondary markets.
stories.impact: Leadership depth expansions in Columbus and Omaha suggest developers expect deal flow and capital activity in these metros—worth monitoring for underlying demand signals (job growth, corporate relocations) that may not yet be in headlines. Market leader elevation typically precedes capital deployment cycles.

stories.title: Greystone Lends $47M for Long Island Section 8 Housing Complex
stories.excerpt: Metropolitan Realty Group has sealed $46.8 million of agency debt for the acquisition and refinancing of a Long Island affordable housing complex, Commercial Observer has learned. Greystone provided the nonrecourse, fixed-rate Fannie Mae-backed loan on the developer’s Pine Town Houses Section 8 garden-style apartment complex in Long Beach, N.Y. The nonrecourse deal, which was originated by Eric Rosenstock, senior managing director at Greystone, and Jeff Englund, executive vice president, carries a 10-year term with five years of interest-only payments followed by a 35-year amortization schedule. “Greystone understands the complexities of affordable housing financing in every market cycle,” Rosenstock said in a statement. Located at 151 East Pine Street, the 1975-built Pine Town Houses property is composed of eight two-story buildings with 130 units ranging from one to four bedrooms. The property, which is supported by long-term Section 8 Housing Assistance Payments, will undergo a “moderate rehabilitation” project as part of the refi, according to Greystone. Greystone also provided Fannie Mae-backed loans of $25.7 million and $19.4 million on Metropolitan Realty Group’s Hudson View II and Hudson View III properties, respectively, in northern Manhattan’s Hamilton Heights neighborhood. Metropolitan Realty Group acquired the portfolio of 129 Section 8 units for $45 million from Aminim Group, New York Business Journal previously reported. Planned upgrades across all three properties include interior apartment renovations, building system improvements and enhancements to common areas, according to Greystone. “The preservation of affordable housing remains one of the most important priorities in our industry,” Scott Jaffee, president of Metropolitan Realty Group, said in a statement. “Every transaction represents an opportunity to help owners secure long-term financing that strengthens their properties, preserves affordability and supports the comm
bullets: Metropolitan Realty secured $46.8M Fannie Mae loan for Long Island Section 8 complex.
130-unit Pine Town Houses in Long Beach, NY, 10-year term, 5-year IO period.
Affordable housing financing remains stable despite broader market cycles.
stories.summary: Metropolitan Realty Group closed a $46.8 million nonrecourse Fannie Mae-backed loan via Greystone for Pine Town Houses, a 130-unit Section 8 complex in Long Beach, NY. The deal featured a 10-year term with five years of interest-only payments and a 35-year amortization, financing both acquisition and moderate rehabilitation of the 1975-built property.
stories.impact: Stable agency lending for Section 8 rentals in Long Island (Queens submarket) confirms continued government-backed multifamily capital availability, even as broader construction lending tightens. This supports development of workforce/affordable units in outer boroughs where land and rents remain cheaper than Manhattan.

stories.title: Skanska Shares Rendering of Bellevue Mass-Timber Office Tower
stories.excerpt: Skanska announced continued progress on Bevel, its planned 12-story mass-timber office tower in downtown Bellevue. The project remains in the architectural design and entitlement phase with the City of Bellevue, as Skanska, Mithun + Waugh Thistleton, and other project partners continue to advance plans for the sustainable office development. Located at the corner of 106th Ave. NE and NE 9th Pl., Bevel is planned as an approximately 200,000-square-foot workplace environment. The project will be adjacent to The Eight — Skanska’s recently completed office tower in Bellevue, which is currently 90% leased. When completed, Bevel is expected to be among the tallest mass-timber office towers in the U.S. and one of the most significant commercial mass-timber office developments in the commercial real estate industry. “Building on the success of The Eight and the strong Eastside market, Bevel represents an incredible opportunity for a future partner to establish a new world-class office or HQ on all levels,” said Charlie Foushée, Executive Vice President and Regional Manager for Skanska USA Commercial Development’s Seattle market. The post Skanska Shares Rendering of Bellevue Mass-Timber Office Tower appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Skanska advancing 200k SF mass-timber office tower in Bellevue, design/entitlement phase.
Bevel planned near The Eight (90% leased), targets tallest US timber office.
Office pipeline in Bellevue shows selective development despite office headwinds.
stories.summary: Skanska is progressing Bevel, a 12-story, 200,000-square-foot mass-timber office tower in downtown Bellevue, with design and entitlement work ongoing. The project is adjacent to The Eight, Skanska's recently completed office tower that is 90% leased, and is positioned as one of the most significant mass-timber office developments in the U.S.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development. Bevel is office-only; however, The Eight's 90% occupancy suggests sustained tenant demand in Bellevue, which could support multifamily absorption in the Seattle Eastside market if office growth attracts or retains talent. Mass-timber innovation in office may influence hybrid residential-office conversions downstream.

stories.title: Report: Domestic Migration Continues Shaping Local Economies
stories.excerpt: Although Florida and Texas are touted as the strongest magnets for in-migration, South Carolina and Delaware actually set the pace last year, Placer.ai says in a new white paper. With the exodus from coastal markets seen during the pandemic having cooled, no state recorded net inflows or outflows exceeding 0.7% of its starting population in 2025. “[[R]return-to-office mandates, higher mortgage rates, and a shrinking affordability gap between coastal cities and many COVID-era hotspots have dampened the incentive to move,” according to Placer.ai’s Migration After the Boom: Where Americans Are Moving in 2026. “But even in a slower market, domestic migration remains one of the most powerful forces shaping local economies, housing markets, and consumer demand.” The new report leverages AI-powered location analytics to examine the relocation patterns reshaping the U.S. It analyzes where Americans are moving, the demographic and economic forces driving those decisions, and how retailers, investors, developers, and policymakers can respond to the opportunities and challenges created by these shifts. The post Report: Domestic Migration Continues Shaping Local Economies appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: South Carolina, Delaware led 2025 in-migration; Florida/Texas pace slowed to <0.7% of population.
Return-to-office mandates, higher mortgage rates, shrinking affordability gaps cool migration.
Domestic relocation remains powerful but decelerating; coast-to-Sun Belt delta narrowing.
stories.summary: Placer.ai reports that South Carolina and Delaware led net in-migration in 2025, while traditional magnets Florida and Texas saw inflows under 0.7% of starting population. Return-to-office mandates, higher mortgage rates, and narrowing cost-of-living gaps between coastal and pandemic-era hotspot markets have dampened the urgency to relocate.
stories.impact: Critical signal for multifamily developers: the pandemic migration boom is over. Demand growth will depend on local job creation and affordability, not national in-migration. Secondary markets (Carolinas, Delaware) and tertiary Sun Belt metros now compete on fundamentals, not tailwinds. Developers must focus on metros with structural job growth (tech hubs, corporate relocations) rather than betting on pure population inflow.

stories.title: Colliers Arranges DC Office Lease for Working America
stories.excerpt: Colliers announced that Working America has signed a 14,354-square-foot lease at 1725 Eye Street, NW in Washington, D.C. The transaction was led by Colliers Executive Vice President Mark Sullivan, Senior Vice President Matt Venos and Client Services Coordinator Cassidy May on behalf of the landlord, Cafritz. Lincoln Property Company represented the tenant. Working America will take occupancy immediately after the current tenant vacates this fall. Owner Calvin Cafritz Enterprises is undertaking a comprehensive capital improvement program designed to elevate the tenant experience. Planned upgrades include a fully modernized lobby, a state-of-the-art conference center, a cutting-edge fitness facility and a redesigned rooftop terrace. “We’re thrilled to welcome Working America and support the impactful work they do for communities across the country,” said Matt Venos, Senior Vice President at Colliers. “With its prime location and ownership’s significant upcoming investment in the property, 1725 Eye Street is an ideal fit.” The post Colliers Arranges DC Office Lease for Working America appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Working America leased 14.4k SF at 1725 Eye Street NW, DC; fall occupancy.
Cafritz investing in modernized lobby, conference, fitness, rooftop at building.
Office leasing in downtown DC continues despite broader sector weakness.
stories.summary: Working America signed a 14,354-square-foot lease at 1725 Eye Street NW in Washington, D.C., with occupancy expected in fall 2026 after current tenant exit. Owner Calvin Cafritz Enterprises is undertaking comprehensive capital improvements including lobby modernization, conference facilities, fitness center, and rooftop renovation.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact. Office leasing activity in DC's core reflects strong nonprofit/advocacy employer base and federal proximity, but does not signal residential demand growth. Capital improvements at Class-B office imply some landlord confidence in tenant retention but not accelerating growth.

stories.title: Associated Bank Completes Financing for Ohio Industrial Building
stories.excerpt: Associated Bank announced the completion of a $6.98 million term loan to Transport Properties and GFH Partners to finance a 75,250-square-foot industrial building located at 3003 Etna Parkway in Pataskala, Ohio, 20 miles west of Columbus. The building, completed in 2022 and leased to Thayer Power & Communication, is situated on 20 acres that includes seven acres of outdoor parking. Transport Properties and GFH Partners own and manage the property. Transport Properties specializes in developing and enhancing industrial outdoor storage properties. GFH Partners is a global real estate asset manager and a subsidiary of the investment bank GFH Financial Group.Elizabeth Hozian, a senior vice president with Associated Bank’s Commercial Real Estate division in Chicago, managed the loan arrangements and closing. Associated Bank’s Commercial Real Estate division has offices in Illinois, Indiana, Michigan, Minnesota, Missouri, Ohio, Texas and Wisconsin. The post Associated Bank Completes Financing for Ohio Industrial Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Associated Bank financed $6.98M term loan for 75k SF industrial building in Pataskala, Ohio.
Transport Properties and GFH Partners own 2022-built property, leased to Thayer Power.
Industrial financing in Columbus submarket supports logistics/manufacturing development.
stories.summary: Associated Bank completed a $6.98 million term loan for Transport Properties and GFH Partners to finance a 75,250-square-foot industrial building at 3003 Etna Parkway in Pataskala, Ohio (20 miles west of Columbus), completed in 2022 and leased to Thayer Power & Communication.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily. Industrial financing in Columbus exurbs reflects steady regional logistics demand but does not indicate residential demand drivers. Monitor if industrial job clusters (e.g., freight, manufacturing) near Pataskala create workforce housing demand downstream.

stories.title: Blackstone Reports Record AUM, Strong Earnings Powered by Data Centers, AI
stories.excerpt: Blackstone’s growth continued unabated in the first half of 2026, as the firm leveraged its enormous data center and AI platform to report strong shareholder returns. In the second quarter, the firm delivered more than 20 percent year-over-year growth across total revenues, fee revenues, fee-related earnings, net realization and distributable earnings, according to Michael Chae, vice chairman and chief financial officer. “The firm’s continued evolution and the expanding scope of our activity have fundamentally transformed our earnings power, both in terms of the magnitude and breadth and sources of earnings,” said Chae. The private equity powerhouse reported that its distributable earnings increased 26 percent year-over-year to $2 billion, with fee-related earnings growing 22 percent to $1.7 billion, or $1.29 per share, and net realizations rising by 27 percent, all despite geopolitical headwinds. Capital inflows into Blackstone reached $70 billion in the second quarter, and now stand at $260 billion in the last 12 months alone, increasing the firm’s total assets under management (AUM) 11 percent year-over-year to a firm-record $1.35 trillion. “Blackstone reported outstanding second-quarter results,” said Blackstone CEO Stephen Schwarzman. “The most significant driver of these strong results continues to be the large-scale investments we’ve made in artificial intelligence-related areas, including data centers, energy and power, and the frontier AI companies themselves.” Schwarzman noted that Blackstone has become one of the biggest private capital providers in the entire AI ecosystem, largely by building what he called “the largest data center development business in the world.” That growth has come through the firm’s 2021 acquisition of QTS Data Centers, and its direct investments into Anthropic, OpenAI, Google and SpaceX. Schwarzman also pointed out that in the second quarter Blackstone launched Blackstone Digital Infrastructure Trust (known by its stock symbol BX
bullets: Blackstone reported 20% YoY revenue growth in Q2 2026, $70B capital inflows.
Data center and AI platform drove distributable earnings +26% YoY to $2B.
Hyperscaler capital spend surge (est. $600B+ in 2026) fuels Blackstone expansion.
stories.summary: Blackstone delivered 20% year-over-year revenue growth in Q2 2026, with distributable earnings rising 26% to $2 billion and capital inflows reaching $70 billion. The firm's data center and AI infrastructure platform was the primary earnings driver, capitalizing on hyperscaler demand for AI compute capacity.
stories.impact: Massive private capital concentration in data centers and AI infrastructure diverts mega-capital from traditional CRE (including multifamily) into a sector with superior returns and less cyclical risk. This tightens financing capacity and raises capital costs for multifamily developers competing for institutional equity. Watch for secondary effects: hyperscaler presence in metros (AWS, Azure regions) drives tech job growth and could lift multifamily demand in select tech hubs, but broad availability of developer capital is constrained.

stories.title: Durst Secures Three Leases at 1133 Avenue of the Americas
stories.excerpt: The Durst Organization has announced three leases totaling 11,223 square feet for prebuilt office suites at the company’s 1133 Avenue of the Americas, Commercial Observer has learned. In the largest of these, software company Quantexa signed a new lease for 5,263 square feet at the building. The U.K.-based firm specializes in artificial intelligence-based software applications for analysis and decision-making, according to the company website. In addition, theater company Olympus Theatricals renewed its lease for 3,345 square feet, and financial services firm Global Securitization Services renewed for 2,615 square feet. The lengths of the three leases were unclear. The Durst Organization was represented in-house by Tom Bow, Rocco Romeo and Nora Caliban. Quantexa was represented by Aidan Campbell of Colliers. “These three leases reflect what tenants are looking for right now: quality space in a best-in-class building,” said Jody Durst, president of the Durst Organization. “We are grateful that Olympus Theatricals and Global Securitization Services have recommitted to 1133 Avenue of the Americas, and welcome Quantexa to the building.” 1133 Avenue of the Americas is a 1.1 million-square-foot, 45-story Class A tower a block north of Bryant Park. Global banking and financial services firm ING Group renewed its 132,400-square-foot space at the building in December 2025, adding 21,515 square feet more for a total of 153,915 square feet throughout the seventh through 11th floors, as reported in CO. Law firm Massumi + Consoli also moved into 22,915 square feet there with a seven-year lease in August 2025. The move was an expansion for the firm, which Durst said now had five times more space than it had in its previous home at Penn 1. The month before, another law firm, Steptoe, signed a 15-year lease at 1133 Avenue of the Americas for 57,988 square feet on the building’s 43rd, 44th and 45th floors. The firm had previously been headquartered at 1114 Sixth Avenue. As of July 2
bullets: Durst Organization closed 11.2k SF in three leases at 1133 Avenue of Americas.
Quantexa (AI software) leased 5.3k SF; Olympus Theatricals, Global Securitization renewed.
Manhattan office leasing continues in Class-A buildings despite sector weakness.
stories.summary: The Durst Organization signed three leases totaling 11,223 square feet at 1133 Avenue of the Americas in Manhattan, including a 5,263-square-foot lease to Quantexa (AI-based software firm) and renewals from Olympus Theatricals (3,345 SF) and Global Securitization Services (2,615 SF).
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily. Manhattan office leasing in Class-A towers reflects strong tenant demand from AI and financial services firms, but office strength does not translate to residential demand absent concurrent population inflow or rent appreciation that incentivizes conversion. Monitor AI sector job growth in NYC as potential residential demand tailwind if sustained.

stories.title: New York’s Temporary Data Center Moratorium Could Mean Permanent Losses
stories.excerpt: On July 14, New York Gov. Kathy Hochul signed the nation’s first statewide moratorium on the building of new hyperscale data centers, pausing all state environmental permits for up to one year. The purpose, according to the governor’s announcement, is to take the time to establish “a nation-leading regulatory framework that protects ratepayers, the environment, the energy grid and communities across the state.” But, at a time when data center construction has become something of a national gold rush, with states competing for the jobs and economic activity new data center activity brings, many are asking if the moratorium will cost New York dearly in terms of economic growth. According to engineering and supply chain intelligence platform Accuris, the top five hyperscalers — Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta and Oracle — are “projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36 percent increase from 2025.” And, for another illustration of just how quickly the industry is evolving, in mid-2025, Goldman Sachs projected that hyperscaler capital expenditure from 2025 through 2027 would reach $1.15 trillion, a massive increase compared to the $477 billion spent from 2022 through 2024. But the investment bank drastically revised its own estimates barely a year later, and now projects that hyperscaler capex in 2027 alone could reach $1.1 trillion. It makes sense, then, that some states are fiercely competing for this spending and the jobs it could bring, and that when a state like New York hits pause, states with more lax regulatory frameworks gain ground on bringing those dollars and jobs home. Texas and Virginia lead the nation in both current data center capacity and the willingness to indulge in as much additional data center construction as possible. According to market intelligence company Cleanview, which tracks large hyperscale data centers with identifiable power capacity, Virginia currently has 371 operating data centers with a combined capacity of
bullets: NY Governor Hochul signed statewide data center moratorium, up to one year pause on permits.
Hyperscalers projected $600B+ 2026 infra spend (36% YoY increase); NY excluded.
Moratorium risks $1.15T hyperscaler capex (2025–27) flowing to competing states.
stories.summary: New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed the nation's first statewide moratorium on hyperscale data center construction on July 14, 2026, pausing all state environmental permits for up to one year to develop regulatory frameworks. Hyperscalers are projected to spend over $600 billion on infrastructure in 2026, a 36% increase from 2025, with Goldman Sachs forecasting $1.15 trillion in capex from 2025–2027.
stories.impact: Critical for New York multifamily: data center moratorium removes a major competitive employer base (tech/AI infrastructure jobs) from NY and redirects it to competing states (Virginia, Texas, Ohio). Limits near-term tech job inflow to NYC, reducing tailwinds for Manhattan and outer-borough residential demand. Loss of data center-driven population and tax revenue may weaken NYS housing market broader support. Developers betting on NYC tech-driven growth should reassess timing and tenant demand assumptions.

stories.title: ESRT Putting 1359 Broadway Up for Sale, Seeking $225M
stories.excerpt: Empire State Realty Trust is parting ways with its mixed-use building at 1359 Broadway and marketing it for sale, sources familiar with the move told Commercial Observer. The property is expected to fetch around $225 million, with the winning buyer owning the fee simple interest in the 486,000-square-foot, 22-story office and retail property at the corner of Broadway and 36th Street two blocks north of Herald Square. The building is a short walk from both Penn Station and Grand Central Terminal and roughly 95 percent leased to a diverse mix of tenants today, with plenty of leasing momentum. Only a few days ago, manufacturer Infinium Wall Systems signed a lease for 30,017 square feet at the property, and the second quarter of 2025, ESRT signed 38,000 square feet of leases between SLCE Architects and nonprofit Braven. As for the retail component, it’s anchored by Wolfgang’s Steakhouse and also includes Japanese restaurant Wokuni. Newmark’s Adam Spies, Josh King, Adam Doneger, Avery Silverstein, Marcela Fasulo and Doug Harmon are leading the sale, sources said, and no debt currently encumbers the property. The sale of 1359 Broadway follows lots of portfolio comings and goings for ESRT. Last month the real estate investment trust (REIT) closed on the $114 million acquisition of the land beneath 111 West 33rd Street and 1400 Broadway. Two months earlier, in April, it went under contract to sell its 26-story office building at 250 West 57th Street to Namdar Realty Group for roughly $280 million. Last, but certainly not least, in December the REIT made headlines when it bought Scholastic’s building at 555-557 Broadway in SoHo for $386 million. The same Newmark team arranged both the 250 West 57th Street and Scholastic building deals. Sources familiar with the moves said ESRT is in the process of upgrading its portfolio and recycling capital in the process. ESRT and Newmark didn’t immediately return requests for comment. Cathy Cunningham can be reached at ccunningham@commer
bullets: Empire State Realty Trust marketing 1359 Broadway for $225M sale.
486k SF, 22-story mixed-use office/retail, 95% leased, near Penn/Grand Central.
ESRT office portfolio exits signal selective capital redeployment from NYC office.
stories.summary: Empire State Realty Trust is marketing 1359 Broadway, a 486,000-square-foot, 22-story mixed-use office and retail building at Broadway and 36th Street in Manhattan, for approximately $225 million in fee simple sale. The property is 95% leased with recent leases to Infinium Wall Systems (30,017 SF), SLCE Architects (18,500 SF), and Braven nonprofit (19,500 SF).
stories.impact: ESRT's office asset sale reflects selective capital redeployment out of Manhattan office, likely to redeploy into logistics, multifamily, or other asset classes. While this particular sale does not directly impact multifamily, it signals institutional landlord caution on NYC office hold-to-maturity and suggests capital may redirect to residential development in secondary markets or mixed-use opportunities. Watch for follow-on ESRT office dispositions that could create conversion or redevelopment opportunities.

stories.title: Alonzo Mourning, HTG net $55M financing package for affordable housing
stories.excerpt: NBA hall-of-famer Alonzo Mourning and Housing Trust Group scored a $55.4 million financing package for a Naranja affordable housing complex. The deal comes as the duo has racked up financing in recent years for below-market rate rentals in Miami-Dade County.  Mourning, through his AM Affordable Housing, and Coconut Grove-based Housing Trust Group plan the 115-unit Artisan Pointe project at 26115 South Dixie Highway in an unincorporated area of south Miami-Dade County. The four-story complex is under construction with completion expected in January 2028, according to the developers’ news release.  The financing package includes $31.5 million in low-income housing tax credit […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Alonzo Mourning/HTG closed $55.4M financing for 115-unit Artisan Pointe in Naranja, Miami-Dade.
$31.5M LIHTC equity, $16.9M permanent loan (Berkadia), $7M CRA funding (TIF/loan).
Affordable housing pipeline in south Miami-Dade validates workforce rental demand.
stories.summary: Alonzo Mourning's AM Affordable Housing and Housing Trust Group secured a $55.4 million financing package for Artisan Pointe, a 115-unit affordable housing complex under construction in Naranja, Miami-Dade County, completing January 2028. Financing includes $31.5 million in LIHTC equity, a $16.9 million Berkadia permanent loan, and $7 million from the Naranja Lakes Community Redevelopment Agency (CRA).
stories.impact: Workforce multifamily demand in south Miami-Dade remains robust enough to attract institutional capital (LIHTC, CRA TIF, permanent debt). At rents of $766–$2,145 for 30–70% AMI households, Artisan Pointe underscores supply gap for sub-market-rate units in rapidly gentrifying south county. Developers should monitor CRA TIF availability and LIHTC pricing in emerging south Dade submarkets; workforce housing pipeline is expanding, signaling sustained demand and institutional willingness to underwrite below-market returns.

stories.title: Endeavor boosts Austin office pipeline with 5th and Walsh project
stories.excerpt: Endeavor’s 5th and Walsh project in Downtown Austin, which will also house its new headquarters, is finally getting off the ground.  Plans for the new five-story, 198,000-square-foot office mid-rise and retail building were submitted to the city in March 2022, more than four years ago. The development will feature 21,000 square feet of restaurant and retail space on the ground floor and a below-grade parking garage, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The project is expected to be completed in the later months of 2028, and Endeavor will promptly move into the building from its old headquarters at 500 West […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Endeavor broke ground on 5th and Walsh in downtown Austin: 198k SF, 5-story office/retail.
50% preleased (85k SF) before shovel; Endeavor HQ + 15k SF retail; completion late 2028.
Amazon advance robotics facility (45-year agreement) in nearby Dog's Head development signals job growth.
stories.summary: Endeavor's 5th and Walsh project—a 198,000-square-foot, five-story office and retail building at 1200 West 5th Street in Downtown Austin—is beginning construction with nearly 50% of space preleased (85,000 SF plus Endeavor's 25,000 SF HQ). The project is expected to complete in late 2028. Separately, Amazon is preparing to build an advanced robotics manufacturing facility in the nearby Dog's Head area (2,614 acres), which Endeavor owns and has signed a 45-year development agreement with the City.
stories.impact: Amazon robotics facility in Dog's Head is a major demand catalyst for Austin residential development. Tech and robotics manufacturing jobs (in addition to existing Amazon HQ presence) will drive inbound migration, household formation, and rental demand across Austin, particularly in central and northwest corridors near Dog's Head. Developers should accelerate entitlement and pre-leasing for multifamily in walkable Austin submarkets (downtown, Central Austin, North Loop) ahead of Amazon facility opening (est. 2027–2029). Strong employment tailwind for Austin apartment absorption and rent growth over next 3–5 years.

stories.title: Starwood files data center plan as Virginia residents revolt
stories.excerpt: Barry Sternlicht’s Northern Virginia data center drive is forging ahead, even as residents plan to revolt against Starwood Capital Group’s dreams. Starwood filed a permit application for its two-building project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria town line in Virginia, the Washington Business Journal reported. The first building, Plaza 500 South, is planned for more than 226,000 square feet, according to the pending application. HKS Inc. is the architect for the project. The first phase of construction is expected to carry a $5 million price tag, according to Fairfax County records. In addition to the two data center […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Starwood Capital (Sternlicht) filed permit for two-building data center at Plaza 500, Alexandria, VA.
226k+ SF Phase 1; $165M purchase price; substation within 100 ft of homes sparks opposition.
Northern Virginia data center corridor faces community resistance; regulatory risk emerging.
stories.summary: Barry Sternlicht's Starwood Capital Group filed a permit application for a two-building data center project at the 34-acre Plaza 500 on the Alexandria–Fairfax border in Virginia, with Phase 1 (Plaza 500 South) planned for 226,000+ square feet. Starwood purchased the site four years ago for $165 million. A proposed on-site substation within 100 feet of homes in the Bren Mar neighborhood has triggered resident opposition and a planned September planning commission rally.
stories.impact: Data center development in Northern Virginia faces NIMBY and environmental headwinds similar to those in New York (see idx 9). Community opposition to substations and grid infrastructure may slow Starwood's Virginia data center pipeline and delay job creation, reducing near-term residential demand inflow. However, if approved, hyperscaler support jobs could drive workforce housing demand in Fairfax County and Alexandria. Developers should monitor local zoning/opposition trends; if data centers advance, suburban multifamily in commutable range (Arlington, Fairfax, Falls Church) may see absorption uptick.

stories.title: Vlad Doronin joining forces with South Korea juggernaut
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s global conquest for the Aman brand is taking the company to South Korea and beyond. Doronin’s OKO Group and the Shinsegae Group, one of the top retail conglomerates in the Asian nation, formed a fund to develop luxury hotels and more, Bloomberg reported. The fund is being launched with an initial $500 million investment. The partners are kicking things off with a mixed-use project in Seoul’s Gangnam District, which will include an Aman hotel, residences and a club, according to Doronin. As they continue to fundraise, the partners will expand out into more of Asia, the Middle East […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Vlad Doronin (OKO Group) partnered with Shinsegae Group for $500M luxury fund.
Initial focus: mixed-use Aman hotel/residences in Seoul Gangnam; expansion to Asia/ME/US.
Aman brand sold $9B in residences in 4 years; 20 hotels under construction globally.
stories.summary: Vlad Doronin's OKO Group and South Korea's Shinsegae Group formed a joint venture fund capitalized at $500 million to develop luxury hotels and residences, with an initial project in Seoul's Gangnam District featuring an Aman hotel, branded residences, and private club. The partners plan to expand into additional Asian, Middle Eastern, and U.S. markets. Aman has sold $9 billion in branded residences in the past four years and has approximately 20 hotels under construction worldwide.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to U.S. multifamily development. However, signals continued institutional capital availability for luxury residential (branded hotels/condos) in select U.S. markets, particularly urban infill. Aman's $9B residential sales volume and global pipeline suggest luxury condo/rental market remains strong; developers competing for limited urban land in tier-1 U.S. cities should monitor Aman/OKO site acquisitions as competitive pressure on land pricing and tenancy mix.

stories.title: Grubb Properties forms $1.9B apartment REIT after recapitalization
stories.excerpt: Grubb Properties wants a bigger balance sheet and bigger financing, forming a multifamily real estate investment trust to that end. The Charlotte-based developer formed a REIT by consolidating several of its funds, Bisnow reported. Link Apartments REIT, which will not be traded or opened up for additional investment, counts 45 properties and more than 5,600 units as part of its portfolio, valued at approximately $1.9 billion. Perhaps the most notable property in the portfolio is 8 Carlisle, a rental tower under construction in Manhattan’s Financial District. This month, Grubb secured a $377 million construction loan for its 462-unit rental project. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Grubb Properties formed Link Apartments REIT: 45 properties, 5.6k units, $1.9B valuation.
8 Carlisle (Manhattan FD) secures $377M construction loan; 462 units, 64-story, 789 ft tall.
Grubb consolidated $617M financing (construction + credit facility) to scale balance sheet.
stories.summary: Grubb Properties created Link Apartments REIT by consolidating multiple funds into a $1.9 billion nontraded trust with 45 properties and 5,600+ units. The flagship asset is 8 Carlisle, a 462-unit luxury rental tower under construction in Manhattan's Financial District, which secured a $377 million construction loan (Maxim Capital $300M, Skylight Real Estate $77M mezz). Grubb also obtained a $240 million credit facility, bringing total financing to $617 million.
stories.impact: Grubb's REIT consolidation and $617M financing raise capital for growth-stage multifamily development. 8 Carlisle construction loan signals strong institutional appetite for tall, luxury rental in Manhattan core despite office weakness, validating mixed-use/rental strategies in dense urban markets. REIT formation enables Grubb to access larger institutional equity and debt, setting competitive template for other mid-sized developers seeking to scale. Monitor Grubb deployment of expanded balance sheet for M&A, site acquisition, or expansion into secondary markets—may signal pricing pressure on developer partnerships and land multiples in next 12 months.

stories.title: NYC’s top construction permits: Week ending July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 New Building: The largest new building application filed last week was on the border of Crown Heights and Prospect-Lefferts Gardens. Rafael Rabinowitz of HSD Construction plans to build a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story development at 103 Empire Boulevard. The mixed-use project is slated to have 50 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Number: B01408304-I1 📊 New Building: Borough Developers filed plans to construct an 82,731-square-foot mixed-use project at 45-51 and 45-49 Vernon Boulevard in Queens. The building will stand 10 stories tall and have 86 apartments. Kao Hwa Lee is the architect. Job Filing Numbers: Q01425664-I1, Q01425663-I1 📊 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: NYC filed 112k SF mixed-use (50 units) at Crown Heights; 86-unit 10-story in Queens.
Bronx filed dual 99-unit towers (128.7k SF) in Tremont; Brookfield major alteration at 250 Vestry.
Multifamily pipeline breadth across outer boroughs signals sustained apartment demand.
stories.summary: NYC construction permits filed in the week ending July 17, 2026 included: a 112,279-square-foot, eight-story mixed-use project with 50 apartments at 103 Empire Boulevard (Crown Heights/Prospect-Lefferts Gardens); an 82,731-square-foot, 10-story mixed-use building with 86 apartments at 45-51 Vernon Boulevard (Queens); and two nine-story apartment towers with 99 units each (128,738 SF combined) in Tremont, Bronx.
stories.impact: Active multifamily permitting across outer boroughs (Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx) confirms sustained apartment demand outside Manhattan core. Dual Tremont towers and Queens development signal developer confidence in Bronx and Queens rents and absorption despite downtown Manhattan office weakness. Permits precede starts by 4–6 months; projects will deliver 2027–2029, supporting supply growth in affordable/workforce segments. Developers should monitor outer-borough rent trends and absorption; if these projects lease-up quickly, vacancy will tighten and rent growth may accelerate, widening arbitrage for new development in less-developed outer-borough submarkets.
